of the board of directors of the Templeton corimuni"y Servj'ces

OUESTI ON

with Mr, chester E. and vida L. FinJ.ey concerning groundlrater

officiaL in question. Director Addy, is involved i.n various

CONCLUS I ON
Director Addy may not participate in any of the District's
decisi.ons concerning groundwater exploration and development on
the Finleys' property. Participation in government decisions
includes discussions, voting, conmitting an agency to a course of
action, determining not to act, entering into a contractual
agreement on behalf of the agency, or using one's officiaL
position to influence the decision in any lray.
FACTS
As noted above, the decision involves the District's
proposal to enter into a contractual relationship with Chester E.
lnd vida L. Finley concerning groundwater exPloration and
development on their property. The proPerty consists of
approximately 180 acres which is located on El Pomar Road in San
LLis obispo -ounty, California. 
If the ground'rater exploration
proves sutcessful, the Dj.strict would drill 
production weIIs and
bay the Finleys for the water produced. The District would pay
tni rinL.ys approximately 54o for each acre foot of water produced
with an annual minimum amount. The rate of Payment per acre foot
would be adjusted annually Pursuant to the california consumer
Price Index and changes in the District's 
r',ater rates' 
The
payment period would be 30 years, and Paynents over that 30 year
p.iioa could exceed a !o!ar of S1,ooo,00o' The District arso
propose= to acquire 4 acres of the Finley property 3t !?ir market
vafue. 
Finaliy, the proposal also prov!'des that the District
wou).d guarantee $/ater service to any future subdivision of the
Finley property, provided that service to any such subdivision
would not involve nore than 30 rneters.
Director Addy has no econonic interest in the Finley
property. He has no business relationships with either chester or
ilial rii.rr.v. 
He does, however, have various business arrangements

interest in the subject ProPerty.

partnership pays rent on the restaurant buiJ.ding to the other
partnership, FYMA Associates.
The shareholders in the Young/Finley Corporation are Bob
Finley and his spouse (50*) and Don Young and his spouse (5ot).
The corporation owns a restaurant in san Luis obispo, rnanages a
restaurant in Tenpleton, and has a 50* partnership interest in the
Main street Grill Restaurant. The corporation is run Iike a
partnership.2 Bob FinIey is its president.
Director Addy and Robert Markwith loaned the Young/Finley
Corporation SlO,OOO each. The loan is evidenced by a promissory
note and an option agreement. If Director Addy and Robert
Markwith elect to exercise their options, they may convert their
Ioan amounts to shares in the Young/Finley Corporation at some
future time. Bob Fi.nley also personally guaranteed the loans.
ANALYSI S
The Political 
Reforxr Act was enacted by the people of the
state of California by in j.tiative in 1974. The purpose for the
conflict-of-interest 
provisions of the Act was to ensure that
public officials, 
whether elected or appointed. would perfor:n
their duties in an impartj,al manner, free from bias caused by
their own financiaL interests or the financial interests of
persons who have supported then. (Section 81001(b).)
fn furtherance of this goal. Section 87100 of the Act
provides:
No public officia] at any level of state or
locaI government shaII make, participate in making
or in any way attempt to use his off j.cia). position
to infLuence a governmental. decision in which he
knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest.
As a director of the Tenpleton Community Services District,
Edward Addy is a "public of f icial." as defined in the Act.
(Section 81048.) Thus, he may not make, participate in making or
use his of f i.ciai position to j.nf luence a governmental decision in
nhich he knohls or has reason co know he has a financial interest'
Making or participatinq in naking governmental decisions has
been interpreted broadly in furtherance of the goals oi the Act,
and includes voting, makinq an appointnent, cornmitting an agency
2 
Th. information concerning the Young/FinLey ccrporation t'ras
provided to me ora.Lly by Karen Hardy, the corporate attorney, on
September 27, Lg9L. She also advised me that the S10,000 loan
from Director Addy to the Young/Finley corporation, which is
evidenced by a note from the corporation, was also personally
guaranteed bY Bob FinleY.

to a course of action, entering into a contractual agreement on
behalf of the agency, determining not to act. negotiating.
advising or naking recommendations to the decision-maker. In
addition, where a public official contacts, or appears before, or
otherwise attempts to influence, any member, officer, 
employee or
consultant of the official's 
agency concerning a governmental
decision, the official 
j.s considered to have used his official
position to influence the decision. (Regulations 18700 and
1azoo.r.) For example, if Director Addy was reguired to
disqualify hirnself from participating in the decisions in question
herein, he would not be able to participate in any of the
discussions as well as in the f inal- vote.
Econonic Interests
sectj.on 87103 specifies that a public of f icial' has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision wil] have a materia] financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the
officiil 
or a member of his or her immediate family3/ or on:
(a) Any business entity in which the PubIic
official 
has a direct or indirect investnent worth
one thousand dollars (S1,000) or nore.
(b) Any rea] property in which the public
official 
has a di.rect or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars (S1,000) or more.
(c) Any source of incone, other than gifts
and other than loans by a conmercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating tr'ro hundred fifty
dollars (5250) or nore in value provided to,
received by or promised to the pubJ'ic official
within 12 months prior to the time $,hen the
decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
official 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any posj.tion of management'
For purposes of this section, indlrect
investment oi interest means any invest::lent cr
3l Section 82029 defines "immediate fami'Iy" to include- an
official's 
spouse and dependent children' 
Thus, an adult son rs
not a mernber of the offilial,s 
inmediate family for purposes cf
the Act.

interest owned by the spouse or dependent chiLd of
a public official, 
by an agent on behalf of a
public officiaL, or by a business entj.ty or trust
in which the official, 
the of f ici.al's agents,
spouse, and dependent children own directly.
indirectly, or beneficialLy a 1o-percent interest
or greater.
Section 87103 (a) - (d) .
In other words, before a conflict of interest can arise
requiring disqualification of a public official, 
an economic
interest of the official 
must be affected by the decision.
According to subdivision (c) of section 87103, where an of f j.cial
makes a loan to another person, the person becomes a source of
promised income for the full anount of the loan until the loan is
repaid.{ Further, the promj.se to repay the Ioan continues as long
as a sufficient anount of tne loan remiins outstanding.5
Thus, j.n the situatj.on you presented, the Young/Fin]ey
corporation, is a source of income to Director Addy for the
balance of the S10,0oo Director Addy loaned to the corporation,
and a potentj.ally disqualifying 'reconomic interest.'r 
Because Bob
Finley personally guaranteed the S10,000 Ioan, he too is a source
of income to Director Addy and a potentially disqualifying
rreconomic interest. "6 consequently, Director Addy may not
partj.cipate Ln any governmental decision if he knows or has reason
to know the decision wiIl, have a reasonably foreseeable material
f inancj.al effect on 8ob Finley.
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it wiII occur. To be reasonably
foreseeabLe, the effect of a decision must be more than a mere
possibility, 
ho$/ever certainty is not required. 
(Downev.cares v'
bownev Comnunitv Developnent Com. (1987) 196 Cal. App. 3d 983,
geg-g4t; witt v. Morrow (19'711 '7O cal. App. 3d 817; In re Thorner
(f975) 1 feeC Ops. 198, coPy enclosed. ) The Act seeks to prevent
more than actual conflicts of interest,' it seeks to prevent even
the appearance of a possible conflict of j.nterest. (witt v'

gro;p of persons acting in concert.

amounting to 5250 or more.

Morrow, supra at 823.) There is no question under the facts
presented here that any decision by the District concerning
groundwater exploration and development on the Finleys' property
will have a financiaL effect on Bob Finley who has a current
beneficial interest in his parents' property which is the subject
of the District's 
decision. That being the case. the next issue
for our consideration is whether the financial effect of the
District's 
decisj.ons on Bob Finley is material.
whether the financiaL effect of a decision is material
depends on the speci.f ic circumstances of each decisi.on. For
example, where a source of incone to the official 
is ,directly
involved" in a decision before the District, 
Regulation 18702.1(a)
provides that the effect of the decision on the source of income
is deemed material and disqualification is required. A person or
business entity is directty involved in a decision before an
official's 
agency when that person or entity, either personally or
by an agent: (1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision
wiII be made by filing an application, cIair,r, appeal, or similar
request or; 
(21 Is a na;ned party in, or is the subject of, the
proceeding concerning the decision before the of f icial' or the
of f ici.al's agency. A Person or business entity is the subject of
a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of any I'icense, pernit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subiect person or business
entity. 
Based on the facts you have provided, neither Bob Finley
nor a business entity of Bob Finley's is directly involved in the
District's 
decisions in question. /
where a source of income is an individual who is not directLy
before the District. 
but may be indirectly affected, Regulation
L8702.6 appl ies :
The effect of a decision is material as to an
individual who is a source of income or gifts to an
official 
if any of the following applies:

income, investments, or other tangible or
intangible assets or liabil'ities 
(other than real'
Property) by sl,000 or more; or

real- property interest in a manner that is

Regulation La102.3 to determine whether the reasonably foreseeable
efiect of the District's 
decisions wiIl be material lrith respect
to Bob Finley. I have enclosed a copy of the regulation for your
convenience. It provides nonetary standards for deter:nining
materiality. 
Those standards state the decision will- be materj.al
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the
decision will be S10,000 or more on the fair rnarket val'ue of the
real property interest, or wiII affect the rental value of the
prope;ty by Sr,ooo or more per 12 nonth period' 
(Regulation
L8702.3; Hallinan Advice Letter, No. A-90-276.)
Based on the information you have provided, it would appear
that the financial effect of the District's 
decisions on the
Finleys' property wilI be material. Thus Director Addy may not
parti-ipate in any of the District's decisions concerning
groundwater exploration and development on the Finleys' property.
Your letter also raises the possibility that Bob Fj.nLey may
be an "economic interest" to Director Addy by virtue of their
partnership relationships in FYMA Associates and Main street
issociates. 
In 1983, in fn re Nord (8 FPPC ops. 6) the Conrmission
addressed a similar question in the context of a limited
partnership. The Commission concluded that an investment by a
timitea partner in a partnershiP constituted an invest:nent
interest in each controlling general partner of the partnership' 8
consequently, a public official 
t as required to disqualify himse)'f
from decisions wnicn vrould foreseeably have a materiat financial
effect on the partnership, or on the general partner as an
individual. 
In addition, a publj'c official 
was required to
disqualify hirnself from decisions whlch would foreseeably have a
rnaterial iinancial effect on any other business entity in which
the general partner acted as a controlling general partner - or
contioJ.ling lhareholder.9 Hovrever. where control of the limited
partnership was vested in three or more general partners,
theoretically none would hold a controlling Posit.ion, and hence
there would te no investment by a limited partner i.n any of them
unJ.ess one or t!,ro were designated as managing general partner(s),
effecEively qivinq them fuII control,r' or one held a 50? or
greater inLeiest. 11
8 An .*""ption was created ror limited partnerships in which the
number of lnvestors exceeded 35 or in which the investors did not
have a preexisting personal or business relationship

controlling general, Partner(s) .

ent ity . "

a limiEed partnership so long as the requisite level of investment

partner, the rationale in Nord would not apply to find that one

lufficient

Associates, the only investment of lrhich is the Iand occupied by
the Main street GrilL Restaurant.
If Bob Finley is, in point of fact, the managj.ng partner,
then Director Addy has an i.nvestment interest in Bob Fin).ey by
virtue of their partnershiP relationships. 
llowever, we need not
analyze this possible "economic interest'r further because we have
alreidy concluded that Director Addy is required to disqualify
himself from the District's 
decisions herein.
I trust this letter has addressed your concerns. Should you
have any further questions regarding this matter. please feel free
to contact me at (916) 322-5901.
